content.text: I need a function that can normalize user inputs into their standardized string names using existing ID and name mappings.

This utility handles normalization by checking if the input is an integer or a string. Depending on the type, it performs lookups in internal data structures to return the corresponding normalized value or confirm its validity.

```python
def normalizeName(self,in_name):
        """
        Takes in an object and normalizes it to its name/string.
        
        Currently, only integers and strings may be passed in, else a :py:exc:`TypeError`
        will be thrown.
        """
        if isinstance(in_name,str):
            assert in_name in self._data["reg"].inv
            return in_name
        elif isinstance(in_name,int):
            assert in_name in self._data["reg"]
            return self._data["reg"][in_name]
        else:
            raise TypeError("Only int and str can be converted to names")
```

The logic strictly limits accepted inputs to integers and strings, raising a TypeError otherwise. It uses assertions to ensure mappings exist before returning results.
If an input string is provided but doesn't exist in the inverse mapping, what specific error will be triggered?
The code will raise an `AssertionError` because of the check `assert in_name in self._data["reg"].inv`. This assertion fails if the name is not present in the allowed strings.
